title: The Oscars Aftermath Mailbag, Plus Neil Jordan on ‘Greta’ | Interview (Ep. 132)
format: Sean and Amanda dig through a reader mailbag on the 91st Oscars' ratings bump, the preferential ballot, and the Green Book win, then Neil Jordan stops by to talk about directing Isabelle Huppert in Greta.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Sean's steelman for even higher ratings: a win might have signaled the show taking a younger audience seriously.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: Its opening Queen medley gets some credit for the ratings bump.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: The eventual Best Picture winner; not knowing it would win until the last moment is one theory for the uptick.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Cited as part of the ratings baseline from last year's show.

films.title: Spider-Man: Far From Home
films.note: Half-joking bait for Amanda's 15-year-old sister (a Tom Holland fan) to actually watch next year.

segments.heading: Best Picture Math: Tentpoles and Endings

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: Jon Favreau's remake, floated as the kind of blockbuster the Academy might start giving "respectability" to.

films.title: Fast & Furious Presents: Hobbs & Shaw
films.note: Sean's joke nomination, not a real prediction.

films.title: Alita: Battle Angel
films.note: Same joke.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Sean doesn't think the franchise-closer will actually get nominated.

films.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
films.note: Untitled "Episode IX" at the time of recording; Sean predicts a nomination as the trilogy's closer, the way Return of the King got in for Lord of the Rings.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Netflix's awards-season centerpiece, reportedly getting a real theatrical push this time.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: Scorsese's prior Best Picture win, name-checked since The Irishman feels like a rerun of that same moment.

segments.heading: Dogs and the Expanded Best Picture Field

films.title: Widows
films.note: The dog-montage question is really a plug for the movie, and for Olivia the dog specifically.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The expanded field's one real surprise winner, looking more anomalous than ever next to Green Book.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Amanda's example of a film she's glad made it in under the eight-nominee system.

segments.heading: Recasting Past Best Picture Winners
segments.note: what a stricter five-nominee, non-preferential ballot might have changed

films.title: Birdman
films.note: Their test case for a film that could have won on a plurality rather than a majority.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: The film they think had the passionate second-place support to flip that year.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: Sean thinks its cluster of support, not consensus, is what pushed Moonlight to the win.

films.title: 12 Years a Slave
films.note: One of the last true neck-and-neck races, alongside Gravity.

films.title: Philomena
films.note: Cited as a film that wouldn't have been nominated in a five-slot field.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Sean says he'd have put it at No. 1 on his own hypothetical ballot regardless of its odds.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: The "generational wound" question: Amanda doesn't buy that a nomination would have changed the show's audience.

segments.heading: Early 2020 Oscar Speculation
segments.note: "wild, baseless speculation just based on names and a logline"

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Quentin Tarantino's Manson-era film; Amanda is excited and dubious about it in equal measure.

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Marielle Heller's Mr. Rogers film, with Tom Hanks playing yet another real person.

films.title: Queen & Slim
films.note: Melina Matsoukas's directorial debut, starring Daniel Kaluuya.

films.title: The King
films.note: Timothée Chalamet as Henry V, from David Michod.

films.title: The Goldfinch
films.note: John Crowley's adaptation; Amanda is 78 pages into the novel.

films.title: Rocketman
films.note: The Elton John biopic; Sean has doubts.

films.title: The Woman in the Window
films.note: Expected as a 2019 release at the time of this taping (it was ultimately delayed to 2021); the Dan Mallory/AJ Finn scandal loomed over its chances.

films.title: The Girl on the Train
films.note: Cited as an underwhelming precedent for a Woman in the Window-style thriller.

films.title: Harriet
films.note: Kasi Lemmons's Harriet Tubman biopic starring Cynthia Erivo.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig's adaptation; one of Sean's most anticipated of the year.

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: Their pick for the snub people will be most appalled by in ten years.

films.title: Sorry to Bother You
films.note: Boots Riley's film, raised as an example of a movie that couldn't compete without a campaign budget.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Olivia Colman's film; proof, per Sean, that a real campaign still mattered even for a winner who barely campaigned herself.

films.title: Blindspotting
films.note: Sean's example for a "best scene" category: he didn't love the film overall but loved a few scenes in it.

segments.heading: Glenn Close, Repeat Winners, and What's Next

films.title: The Wife
films.note: Close's nominated film. She was the prohibitive favorite despite it being nearly impossible to see before January.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Fallout
films.note: A listener's pitch for a lifetime-achievement Tom Cruise nomination; Sean doubts the Academy bites.

films.title: Moonstruck
films.note: Sean's model for where Lady Gaga's career could go: Cher bounced between music and acting for decades.

films.title: Greta
films.note: Jordan's paranoid thriller starring Isabelle Huppert and Chloë Grace Moretz; he reframed the script as an urban Hansel and Gretel once Huppert signed on.

films.title: The Crying Game
films.note: One of the films Sean calls out as a personal favorite; Jordan says the anniversaries keep sneaking up on him.

films.title: The Good Thief
films.note: Sean's other personal favorite from Jordan's filmography.

films.title: Michael Collins
films.note: Jordan's answer when asked about a genre he's already tackled: his one war movie.

films.title: Angel
films.note: Jordan's directorial debut.

films.title: The Company of Wolves
films.note: His first fairy-tale film, drawn from Angela Carter's reworking of familiar stories.

films.title: Mona Lisa
films.note: Jordan's own reference point for Greta: a noirish thriller, though less of a "slushy, stalkery" one.

films.title: Rebecca
films.note: The only Hitchcock film Jordan says he actually thought about while making Greta.

films.title: Vertigo
films.note: The other Hitchcock film he sees sharing Rebecca's register of doomed romanticism.

films.title: Repulsion
films.note: Polanski's claustrophobia study, a touchstone for Greta's locked-room dread.

films.title: The Vanishing
films.note: George Sluizer's film, another claustrophobia reference point Sean brings up.

films.title: Interview with the Vampire
films.note: Nearing its 25th anniversary at the time of this conversation; Jordan recently rewatched it with an audience.

films.title: Excalibur
films.note: John Boorman's film. Working on its final draft as Boorman's assistant is how Jordan got into movies.

films.title: Velvet Buzzsaw
films.note: Jordan's example of a well-made film that vanished the day it dropped on Netflix.

films.title: Chinatown
films.note: Jordan's pick for a screenplay that reads like a great novel someone forgot to write.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: His answer for the best screenplay ever written.

films.title: Sex, Lies, and Videotape
films.note: Cited as the film that kicked off the independent film boom Jordan came up in.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: The film Jordan says that same indie boom "peaked" with.

films.title: A Quiet Place
films.note: Jordan's pick for the last great thing he's seen, praised for its simplicity and its brutal opening.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: The other recent film he calls truly special, for Paul Schrader's refusal to move the camera.

Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker

Birdman.title: Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance)

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
